title: Cat Constipation Guide: Straining and Dry Stool Triage
meta_desc: Is your cat straining to poop? Learn about feline constipation, why dry stool occurs, and when to seek veterinary care. Urgency Level: Medium.
content_html:
TL;DR: Feline constipation causes painful straining and requires a veterinary visit within 24 hours, though an inability to urinate is a life-threatening emergency requiring immediate care.

What is feline constipation and why is it happening to my cat?

When a cat strains to defecate and produces dry, hard stools, it is typically a sign of constipation. This condition occurs when waste moves too slowly through the digestive tract, causing the colon to absorb too much moisture. This makes the stool difficult and painful to pass. It can be caused by dehydration, low-fiber diets, or underlying health issues like kidney disease.

Is my cat’s constipation a medical emergency?

  • Urgency Level: Medium. While mild constipation is not usually an immediate life-threatening emergency, it requires a veterinary exam within 24 hours to prevent complications like megacolon.
  • Critical Warning: If your cat is straining but not producing anything, it is vital to ensure they are not suffering from a urinary blockage, which is a critical emergency, especially in males.

How can providing a photo or video help my veterinarian triage my cat?

  • Capture a photo of the stool (if any is produced) to help the veterinarian assess the exact consistency of the feces.
  • Record a video of the straining behavior so the vet can assess the severity and duration of the effort.
  • These visuals help your vet differentiate between simple constipation, colitis, or more serious physical obstructions.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

The classic clinical signs of constipation in cats are tenesmus and the passage of firm, dry feces. On physical examination, abdominal palpation and rectal examination can confirm the presence of large volumes of retained fecal matter. Affected cats may also exhibit lethargy, depression, anorexia, vomiting, and abdominal discomfort. Plain abdominal radiographs may help establish the inciting factors of fecal retention. Dietary adjustment with ready access to water and high-fiber diets is recommended, with psyllium (1-4 tsp/meal) or pumpkin (1-4 tbsp/meal) added to canned food. It is important to ensure the cat is well-hydrated before starting fiber supplementation to minimize the potential for impaction of fiber in the constipated colon.

Chapter: Gastroenterology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 381)

faqs.question: Can I give my cat a laxative at home?
faqs.answer: You should never give your cat human laxatives or home remedies without a veterinarian's direct advice, as some can be toxic or cause severe dehydration.

faqs.question: How do I know if it's a urinary blockage instead?
faqs.answer: A cat with a urinary blockage will often visit the litter box frequently, cry out in pain, and may lick their genital area excessively. If no urine is being produced, seek emergency care immediately.

faqs.question: What are common signs of discomfort in a constipated cat?
faqs.answer: Aside from straining, you may notice a decreased appetite, lethargy, or vomiting if the constipation is becoming severe.
